category: Related product card contains conflicting coffee specs

consistency.notes: All non-null, non-empty ground-truth fields are supported by the page text. 'coffee.roaster_country' = 'USA' is supported indirectly by the footer location 'Duluth, Minnesota' and shipping text 'Free US shipping'. Numeric price and package size match the selected/default 250g variant shown on the page and embedded variant data.

realism.reasons: Very plausible as an html-to-text render of a real specialty coffee product page. The structure matches common Shopify-style roaster pages: header nav, product title, price, size selector, purchase CTA, concise spec block, descriptive sourcing paragraph, tasting notes, brew suggestion, related products, shipping note, variant data, and footer. The coffee language is natural and on-brand for specialty roasting—origin, region, producer, washing station, process, variety, altitude, and harvest are presented coherently, and the tasting notes/roast intent read like real copy rather than generic filler. The Rwanda details are internally consistent: Red Bourbon, washed process, raised-bed drying, high-elevation smallholders delivering to a washing station in Northern Province all fit the category. The variant block also looks realistic, with IDs, SKUs, package weights, pricing, and availability. Minor possible tell: Harvest 'July-August 2025' is a bit specific/future-dated depending on context, but not enough to feel fake. Overall this is close to indistinguishable from a real roaster product page.

label.coffee.sensory_text:
Tasting Notes: cranberry, orange blossom, panela, black tea

In the cup we taste tart cranberry up front, a floral orange-blossom lift, and panela sweetness that settles into a black-tea finish. We roast this lot lightly to keep the citrusy structure and delicate florals intact.
label.coffee.display_tasting_notes: cranberry, orange blossom, panela, black tea
label.coffee.producer_text: This clean, high-elevation Rwanda is from smallholder growers delivering ripe Red Bourbon cherry to Gitwe Hill washing station in Gakenke. The lot is built from day lots separated for density and sweetness, then fully washed and dried slowly on raised beds.
